posts.title: Fallout: How to run a hardware program correctly
posts.summary: A stark contrast between two Hack Club hardware programs

figures.text: As of writing this blog post, I have attempted to participate in two Hack Club hardware YSWS programs, Blueprint and Fallout. They are both Hack Club hardware programs, but the similarities end there. Blueprint is complexity based, Fallout is hours based. Blueprint's flagship event was in San Jose, and Fallout's flagship event will be in Shenzhen. And my experience with Blueprint was abysmal but my experience with Fallout has been mostly smooth so far. But the issues I had with blueprint aren't isolated to Blueprint. In fact, these problems started with Highway, Hack Club's first ever large scale hardware program and have crept from iteration to iteration up until Fallout.

A YSWS, short for "You ship, We ship" is a program most commonly seen in Hack Club where participants spend an amount of time building a project and receive a monetary or material grant to build the project in real life, fly to a flagship hackathon, or simply claim some prizes. In large scale YSWS programs like Fallout, work hours are converted to a currency (in Fallout's case, Koi) and are able to be spent on a collection of prizes.

figures.caption: A collection of prizes offered at Fallout

content.title: The problems with existing hardware programs

figures.text: Let's say you're developing a game that you wish to submit to a game jam. The jam organizers provide some quick start tutorials, but all of them are for platformers and you want to build a puzzle game. Fine you guess, you'll learn it on my own. You spend hours polishing my gameplay loop, creating animations, and building interesting levels. Now the game is ready to submit. So you submit your project to the game jam and wait three days for the results, only for your game to be rejected because it's "not complex enough," not that the game isn't fun to play, but the code is too simple. OK fine, you can still submit it to a lower tier in the same game jam, so you do that. Wait another three days and low and behold, it gets rejected again because you didn't add a lives system, which every game is required to have.

You reread the requirements for projects and at no point does it say you need a lives system anywhere. At this point, you're getting pretty pissed so you decide to just implement the stupid lives system, adding feet to a snake, and resubmit. Seven days go by without a response and you think your game is finally accepted, only for it to be rejected again because you used an older version of a game engine instead of the new install.
This is how Hack Club hardware programs used to operate. One sided tutorials, vague and unnecessary project restrictions, and complexity based grants.

content.title: How complexity based grants kill cool projects

figures.text: Blueprint, and the majority of Hack Club hardware programs for that matter, use complexity based grants instead of hour based grants which just like peer voting, seems like a good idea until you think about it for five minutes, and the best way I can explain this is by showing what happens when this gets applied to other programs.

What even counts as complexity? For something like a game or website, the code is the only technical aspect of it so complexity is easier to judge but for hardware there are three separate axes of complexity: mechanical, electrical, and software. Most hardware programs have adopted the definition of electrical and software complexity,

Complexity is the wrong way to judge a hardware project. Talk to any experienced CAD designer and they'll tell you that a simple mechanism that works is better than a complex one that doesn't. You wouldn't do something in twelve steps if you know you can do it in two. There are so many more things that matter for a hardware project such as

items: How well does it solve that problem
How much effort was spent on it

figures.text: In the Blueprint program, of the eight guided projects on the Blueprint website, only two of them even include CAD instructions at all. But something that the program never fails to instruct is PCB design. All eight of the guided projects plus the vast majority of custom projects included some kind of PCB. While Blueprint never outright required PCBs in projects, they overpromoted it to the point where it was essentially the only kind of project getting any high tier approval.

A common excuse for shoving PCBs into every corner of Hack Club hardware is, to quote a hardware program lead, "PCBs naturally come up because they're the easiest way to accomplish the polished bar for anything beyond a circuit." And yes, PCBs are wonderful WHEN THE DESIGN IS FINALIZED AND CONFIRMED WORKING. If you force a couple hundred teenagers who have never touched electronics in their life to design a PCB, chances are they're going to make a mistake. With a breadboard, this could easily be fixed by simply moving the wires but a printed PCB requires an entire design iteration, reprint, and another 12 days of shipping.

figures.quote: I think hardware-based YSWSs should be much less focused on creating PCBs and more on the actual circuit creation process. [...] I believe focusing more on beginner circuit creation and iteration through kits and individual parts, rather than entire PCBs that are hard to customize after manufacturing and are expensive, would help hack clubbers actually learn how things work rather than copying a tutorial they do not understand.

figures.text: In reality, a PCB does not determine if a project is polished or not. Using product quality as a bar for polish, a standalone PCB is actually on the lower end of the scale. It's only when that PCB is part of a larger project that it becomes polished, in which case it's the quality of the larger project that makes it polished, not the PCB.
The overpromotion of PCBs pairs with the neglect of mechanical or microcontroller CAD projects.

is a very common piece of advice given for sourcing components, and it was part of the reason my blueprint project was rejected for the second time. It is also a terrible piece of advice. Aliexpress has components that are cheaper than other storefronts, but it's not as simple as "everything is cheaper there."

A large number of extremely cheap Aliexpress listings are scams, something I have personal experience with when I attempted to order cheap filament. Many components have a limit of one per person, making it impractical to purchase a reasonable quantity of them for a project. Even more components are the same price or higher on Aliexpress when you account for shipping and the volume you actually need. In practice, only a select few electronics are actually cheaper on the platform, and the cost savings is usually pennies on the dollar through the scope of an entire project.
Fallout

posts.title: How to name a game
posts.summary: A few common naming styles for indie games

figures.text: If you're like me, chances are you've procrastinated on naming your game and struggled to come up with a good name with hours or even minutes left before a game jam submission deadline. To quote Fireship, "There are only two hard things in programming: naming things and coming up with original jokes." and I've personally struggled to start several projects just because I couldn't think of what to name my GitHub repository. So after developing (and naming) games for two years, here are the approaches I find that work the best.

content.title: Approach 1: What is your game's main mechanic?

figures.text: This is by far the easiest way to name a game, taking the main mechanic of the game and turning that into the name. Since you developed the game and are familiar with the main mechanic, these names are often what you think of when first thinking of a name, and in most cases they work perfectly fine. A benefit of main mechanic names is that upon reading the name, the player immediately knows what the game is about.  Some examples of games named after their main mechanic include

This often works well when the main mechanic is novel or defines how the game works, but can backfire quickly when applied to common mechanics, such as in simulator games where you have thousands of games all named in some form "something followed by simulator." In these cases, it's often better to use a different name style.

content.title: Approach 2: Who or what is your main character?

figures.text: This is the style most common historically and it works reliably because the characters are one of the main defining features of the game, and, unlike the mechanics, are often distinct across games or series. These names are ubiquitous in story based games because they work well when the game's primary defining feature is the characters. But these names work universally, even in games without a clear character since they introduce an aspect of gameplay such as how the game "The Witness" (mild spoiler ahead) requires the players to witness scenes from different angles to solve puzzles. Some examples of main character games include

items: Micro Builders (also one of my games)

content.title: Approach 3: What is the first thing someone playing your game will encounter?

figures.text: The goal of a game title is to 1: get the attention of the player who is likely browsing through a wishlist with dozens of games on it and 2: to offer a quick insight into how the gameplay works. Since the player doesn't know what the game's mechanics are before playing it, the first encounter approach is a difficult one to pull off which is why it is uncommon by itself.

This works best when the first encounter accurately represents the atmosphere of the game, such as how in the game "Papers, Please," the inspector begins every inspection with that same phrase. Additionally, a gamer hearing "Papers, Please" can immediately tell that the game is about inspecting documents at a border crossing, a mental image the game wouldn't get if it were called "The Inspector" because what exactly ARE we inspecting?

content.title: Approach 4: What does your game feel like?

figures.text: This is one of the hardest to execute, but when done correctly it establishes the best mental atmosphere for what the game feels like, that's kind of the entire goal here. This is difficult because some games can feel completely different during different phases during the game.

A good way to pull this off is by asking yourself "What does my game look like in the midgame, after some gameplay?" For example, a city building game would have a skyline after a few hours of gameplay, hence the name Cities: Skylines. Or a game about building and upgrading subway lines would end with creating a Mini Metro.

Some web based games use the domain they are hosted at as the name of the game. The .io games are famous for this naming convention, but only web based games use domains so this is not applicable to most games.
Another approach is to use a pun or pop culture reference in the name. I wouldn't consider this a full approach though because it's relatively uncommon.
The best games often don't use a single approach in isolation but combine multiple approaches to create an evocative title that remains memorable even after hours of gameplay. Some of these include

Ultimately, there is no "best approach" for naming games, and it all comes down to what your game is about. One method that often works is just to try every approach and see which one sounds the best. Ask your friends what they think your game is about from the title, and make sure it isn't too similar to other games.

posts.title: Card Showcase: Rich Blog Layouts
posts.summary: A demonstration post that exercises every supported blog card type.

figures.text: This card shows the new baseline structure: each blog block is now a card with an optional title and a list of content figures.
That means a post can move from plain prose into images, charts, timelines, and other visual formats without introducing a separate template for each section.

items: Titles are optional on every card.
Each card can contain multiple figures.
Legacy text posts still render by being converted into text cards.

figures.alt: Hackathon participants standing near a presentation area.
figures.caption: A single hero-style image works well when one photo should carry the section.

figures.alt: Opening presentation at Campfire San Jose.
figures.caption: Opening talk

figures.alt: Trophies lined up on a table.
figures.caption: Awards table

figures.alt: Winners standing together after the event.
figures.caption: Closing photo

figures.title: Bar
figures.description: Projects launched by quarter.

figures.title: Line
figures.description: Weekly traffic after a homepage refresh.

figures.title: Pie
figures.description: Where demo requests came from.

figures.title: Scatter
figures.description: Response time versus signup conversion.

figures.title: Table
figures.description: Campaign snapshot in table form.

figures.title: Radar
figures.description: How the launch scored across six dimensions.

figures.title: Builds shipped
figures.description: Internal deploys completed before launch week ended.

figures.title: Reviewer completion
figures.description: Share of post sections that received a final pass.

figures.title: Partners onboarded
figures.description: External groups who received the new materials package.

figures.quote: A flexible publishing system should let the content decide the layout, not the other way around.

figures.title: Text-only section
items: Weak at showing data or visual evidence.
Forces every idea into the same presentation shape.

figures.title: Card-driven section
items: Lets each idea use the format that fits it best.
Keeps stats, media, and structured answers readable.
Makes long posts easier to scan without losing depth.

figures.label: Hack Club
figures.description: A community reference that often appears in Magma Labs event and hackathon discussions.

figures.label: GMTK Game Jam
figures.description: An example of a large event where judging format and submission scale both matter.

figures.title: Kickoff
figures.description: The post structure was switched from section-only prose to reusable content cards.

figures.title: Media pass
figures.description: Images and visual blocks were added so the article could show evidence instead of only describing it.

figures.title: Wrap-up
figures.description: The example post finished with charts, embeds, and structured Q and A blocks to prove the full system end to end.

figures.question: Do older posts need to be rewritten immediately?
figures.answer: No. Older section-based content is converted into text cards automatically, so existing posts keep working.

figures.question: Can one post mix multiple figure types in one article?
figures.answer: Yes. The showcase above combines prose, images, charts, stats, quotes, comparisons, embeds, a timeline, and Q and A in a single post.

posts.title: Why I Quit Competitive Math
posts.summary: My whole life in 2023, completely gone in 2026

figures.text: In 2023, competitive math was my entire academic life. My only extracurricular consisted of participating in my school's MATHCOUNTS team, taking the AMC 10 every year and hoping I qualify for AIME, and attending math classes on the side. But today, competitive math has been wiped from my existence. In fact, I haven't participated in a math extracurricular in nearly a year. How does something that was once my entire life completely disappear in the span of three years?

Ever since I was a kid, I had always had some interest in math. I remember distinctly playing with my grandmother's calculator, hitting x2 repeatedly and memorizing the powers of two up to 1048576. I memorized other numbers too, including my mom's library card number and pi to a hundred digits. In third grade math class, I was working on a multiplication problem where I was asked to multiply 24x26. I worked out the product by hand and I noticed that the result was exactly one smaller than 25x25. There was a similar problem on the homework where I was asked to multiply 16x14 and again, the product came out to be exactly one smaller than 15x15. By the third iteration, I had stopped computing the product altogether and switched fully to using the shortcut that I now know as the difference of squares.

My parents quickly picked up on my passion for math and being from a community where education came first, I took my first afterschool math olympiad class in fourth grade. Over the next few years, I took several math courses at several different math schools learning basic algebra, geometry, number theory, and probability.

Sixth grade was the first time I participated in AMC. I took the AMC 8 and 10 and achieved the scores of 16 and 63 respectively. Sixth grade was also the first time I participated in my school's MATHCOUNTS program. Of the 30 students enrolled in the program, only I and three others were sixth graders, all three of whom I still have relationships with today. During sixth grade and the subsequent year, I participated in this program, learned additional concepts from online classes, and attempted the AMC every year.

In eighth grade, after skipping it the previous year, I decided to reattempt the AMC10. Looking at the previous contest scores on AoPS, I decided to aim for a score of 105 which would require answering 15 questions correctly and leaving the remainder blank, and I was achieving this score on practice tests. The day of the test comes and taking the test, I had time to answer 18 problems. When the results were posted online, I had achieved a score of 112.5, with 17 correct answers, a single incorrect answer, and seven omitted problems. Incidentally, 112.5 was the exact cutoff for the honor roll or top 5% on that problem set and my original goal of 105 was the exact AIME qualification cutoff. I took the AIME that year but didn't perform particularly well, earning a slightly below average score of 4. But by this point, a more systemic problem than scores began to rear its ugly head.

I initially enjoyed math because of the discovery and problem solving aspect: Knowing the powers of two allowed me to easily calculate Minecraft stacks in my head. Knowing basic logic allowed me to beat puzzle games, starting with minesweeper and moving on to Tetris and then Baba is You. And noticing that two numbers differing by two multiplied out to be exactly one less than the average squared became a shortcut I abused on every third grade math homework assignment. Math 𝘸𝘢𝘴 a puzzle game.

In fact, as many concepts I learned in math classes, I discovered an equal or greater number on my own from reading books and solving problems. Applying the pattern from Pascal's Triangle gave me the solution to number-of-path problems and a shortcut to binomial expansion. A point on my initial AIME qualification can be attributed to discovering geometric probability. Even Markov chains, the very piece of math that powers AI today, I rederived on my own to solve probability problems. But one thing stayed the same: I participated in competitive math because I enjoyed it, not because it was good for college applications.

The first time I qualified for AIME, this puzzle game version of math disappeared. Now that my talent in math was proven, I was expected to qualify for USAMO in high school which would supposedly earn me a guaranteed T20 admission. Discovering new and interesting facts became grinding mock tests for months to see a three point improvement. Learning one method to solve several problems became optimizing several methods to potentially solve one.

Interviews of previous USAMO qualifiers reveals that they devoted at a minimum of three hours a day, essentially a year round sport, to grinding math problems. Kevin Huang, a USAMO qualifier, dedicated three hours to math every weekday and eight hours on weekends, accounting for essentially all of his free time. I loved math, but what I don't love is solving the same problems for hours every day, day after day, month after month, just to potentially qualify for one competition. Couple that with my discovery of school clubs, cross country, and hackathons, and suddenly competitive math just wasn't that appealing anymore.

figures.title: Hours on weekdays
figures.description: All remaining free time after school, homework, and sleep

figures.title: Hours on weekends
figures.description: Essentially the entire waking day

figures.title: Hours per week
figures.description: More than most sports. Nearly a full time job.

figures.text: I began to decrease my involvement in math starting second semester of ninth grade, and by the start of tenth, AMC was the only math competition I participated in, and I did so without preparation or expectations. Several of my friends from MATHCOUNTS continued competitive math into high school, but by their second failed JMO qualification, they had began to lose hope. To quote one of them, "Its the most depressing [stuff] ever, you just gamble and then lose." It turns out, the reason USA(J)MO qualification is so prestigious is because of how difficult it is. There is no clean AMC -> AIME -> JMO path, qualifying for one gives no guarantee of qualifying for the next level regardless of effort.

figures.quote: Its the most depressing [stuff] ever, you just gamble and then lose.

figures.text: If you observed my life three years ago, you would see me solving math problems in my free time. But if you observed my life today, you would find me building projects. Over my last year of not being involved in competitive math, I've learned Unity game development, Onshape CAD, DaVinci Resolve video editing, yearbook layout using Adobe Creatice Cloud, and many other creative tools. I've become the president of my school's Game Development Club, joined my school's FIRST robotics competition team, and lead organized a hackathon in the Bay Area, one with a 90% participant satisfaction rate, 20 points higher than the national average. But beyond everything else, I find personal projects emulate the "discovery puzzle game" feeling the best for a slew of reasons including

items: Projects can vary significantly in scope, I can build a game in four hours or four months
Every project is unique and requires different skills from the ones before it
I can interact with the projects that I build way more than I feel connected with the problems I solve on paper
I can exercise my creative freedom in projects way more than in anything else
Projects can be paced based on how I am feeling, there is no "I have to grind this so I can do well on that"

figures.text: Competitive math kept me alive through middle school, the darkest time of my life, but personal projects allowed me to bring my ideas to life, from a simple tetris game to a full sized rail mounted vehicle. From my first hackathon to the flagship Shipwrecked hackathon on an island. Personal projects have taken me further in a year than competitive math ever has.

figures.caption: The Shipwrecked Hackathon on an Island in the Boston Harbor. I am visible in the bottom left.
figures.title: Shipwrecked Hackathon

posts.title: The Flawed Nature of Peer Voting
posts.summary: Why Unregulated Peer Voting is an Abysmal Judging System.

content.heading: Introduction
paragraphs: As of writing this blog post, I have been involved in the organization of two hackathons and I've participated in a good number of them myself. Hackathons often come with prizes, but to determine who wins those prizes, it needs to be determined which projects are of the highest quality. This is often done in two ways: either professional judges review each and every project and hand pick the best one, or the projects are released to the participants and each participant is allowed to vote on which projects should win.

My first hackathon, MelonJam 5 used a judging system to determine winners but included a community vote prize for the game voted first place overall on the itch.io jam page. The jam allowed any one submitter to rate any other submission on categories such as gameplay, theming, and polish from one to five stars. On the surface, this seems like a great way to allow the community to participate in the democratic process of selecting the winners but if you think about it for more than five minutes, you start to discover all kinds of issues.

content.heading: Where peer voting breaks
paragraphs: The vast majority of traditional hackathons use a professional judging system. Oftentimes the judges are representatives sent by sponsors or the organizers themselves. Peer voting as a judging system began to rise in popularity mainly within Hack Club hosted global satellite hackathons such as Counterspell, Scrapyard and Daydream. Hack Club has also been known to use peer voting to determine project quality in several programs such as Summer of Making.

The fundamental flaws of the peer voting system stem from the fact that participants would not be able to judge projects fairly due to personal biases and conflicts of interest. Every participant, even those who attend an event just for the experience, wants to win a prize. When given a vote, some participants will vote fairly, but the desire to win can take others down a more nefarious path. Participants can and will "trade votes" with other teams in order to boost their own chances of winning prizes, something I have personally witnessed at Scrapyard Silicon Valley. In other cases like MakerHacks 1, I have seen participants of the Open Prototype (traditional hackathon) track begging robotics participants (who were allowed to vote in the open prototype voting form) to vote for their project, making the judging less about project quality and more about advertising.

content.heading: Review bombing and bias
paragraphs: While advertising and trading votes can significantly influence the results of a ballot judged hackathon, when the voting system is not casting ballots but rather rating each and every project on a scale such as in the case of itch.io game jams, the potential for nefarious influence significantly increases. Properly testing, rating, and reviewing a project takes time, which makes it impractical for a participant to rate every project. However, it is extremely easy for a nefarious actor to spam one-star ratings on many projects to decrease the average rating of other projects and make their own look better in comparison. This spamming of low ratings is known as "review bombing" and even a small percentage of review bombers can destroy honestly voted ratings to the point where they become effectively useless for any kind of judging.

Some may be quick to point out that review bombing will have the same effect on every project in the event. This is obviously an incorrect statement as some projects receive less ratings than others due to incompatible platforms, a steeper learning curve, or general randomness, meaning a review bomb will have far worse effects on projects with less ratings.

Even if a participant is trying to vote ethically, it is effectively impossible for them to make a completely fair vote. In large scale events, a participant may have dozens or even hundreds of projects to choose between and even if they wanted to, they simply would not have the time to carefully review every project. Soft barriers such as a lack of thumbnail images or tutorial or hard barriers like platform incompatibility can hinder a participant's attempt to test and rate a project. The limited amount of time to review projects ultimately leads to votes being determined by first impressions instead of overall functionality. Logical biases such as the familiarity and availability heuristics can cause participants to be biased toward projects made by teams working near them. Ultimately all of these factors combined lead to biased results not caused by nefarious intent but by the imperfection of human nature.

content.heading: Why judges are stronger
paragraphs: Professional judges are also affected by many of the same logical biases that affect participants, but the effect is mitigated significantly due to a judge's exposure to much more projects. While judges may have biases favoring a certain type or style of project, they do not have any personal interest in manipulating the results. A judge's entire role is to judge projects, so they are able to review more projects with more time for each project, allowing them to judge projects more fairly. Professional judges have far more knowledge about the industry they're employed in, so they can most accurately determine which projects are of the highest quality. It is nearly impossible to nefariously influence the outcome of a professionally judged hackathon. Professional judging is not a perfect system, but it is far less biased and unfair as peer voted results.

The best and most obvious solution, the one that the majority of hackathons have implemented, is simply not to use peer voting. This was and still is the case in MelonJam and in essentially every hackathon not affiliated with Hack Club.

content.heading: A practical compromise
paragraphs: In extremely large scale events such as the GMTK Game Jam, the number of submissions makes a pure judging system extremely impractical, and purely voted results would contain all the issues mentioned above. Instead, GMTK uses a hybrid system where entries enter a round of community voting and the top 100 games in the jam, and the judge will play those 100 games to determine 20 winners.

When peer voting is absolutely required, actions can be taken to improve the fairness of the process. These actions include but are not limited to: requiring votes to include a review of the project, allowing organizers to disqualify projects that are asset or template rips, and automatic detection and removal of review bombs.

However, in smaller events such as Daydream Global and smaller satellite hackathons, there really is no excuse to not involve judges in some part of the process other than "we don't want to". MelonJam is judged entirely by current and former members of the Cupertino Game Development Club. Each officer is assigned a set of games to judge and told to pick three favorites. The selected projects are combined to enter a final discussion with all judges, and winners are selected. Daydream Global could have easily been professionally judged with even a small team (say eight judges judging 25 games each) of judges, bypassing all of the inevitable problems of peer voting. Instead, we got a single category of judge prizes with the vast majority of prizes being determined by a fundamentally flawed system.

posts.title: Why "Closed Source" ≠ "Bad"
posts.summary: The Composition Fallacy and All-or-Nothing Thinking

content.heading: Introduction
paragraphs: One of the most commonly repeated statements within the open source hacker community has got to be "Anything that isn’t open source is automatically the worst thing to ever exist." And while you won’t likely hear anyone utter those exact words, members of this community often refuse to recommend Bambu Lab 3d printers, Mac Laptops, or even Unity Engine simply because they aren’t open source.

Some will even go as far as to claim that users of closed source products aren’t "real hackers" and that all "real hackers" use a 12 year old ThinkPad, or even better, a PC. But while closed source software isn’t a deal breaker for the majority of the population, this argument is part of a larger systemic problem of all-or-nothing thinking.

content.heading: What causes all-or-nothing thinking
paragraphs: Human brains aren’t perfect and our minds don’t fully process everything we perceive - our brains take shortcuts to give us what looks like a full picture but don’t contain the full details. But more importantly, we as humans are being continuously shaped by what we learn and the behavior of those around us, namely education and politics.

From the first day of school to the day you graduate, your performance at school is graded on a letter scale with A being an excellent score and F being failing. At least, that’s how it works in theory. From 1985 to 2020, the average high school GPA in the United States has risen from 2.6 to 3.1 with the percentage of A’s increasing even sharper. (https://www.nea.org/nea-today/all-news-articles/grade-inflation-real-it-real-problem)

content.heading: The composition fallacy in practice
paragraphs: In academically competitive regions like the Bay Area, grading essentially functions on a pass-or-fail system: an A is a pass and a B or lower is a fail, an all-or-nothing system. But even in areas where academics are less important, parents have their own standards of what grade is acceptable and oftentimes, that standard is a fixed number across all subjects. For example, if a parent’s standards were for their child to get a minimum of B across all subjects, a student with A’s in math, science, and English but a C in history would be treated as a failure. However, a student with a B in all subjects would meet their parents standards even though their average grade is lower than the first example.

When multiple layers of all-or-nothing thinking stack together, what ends up happening is known as the fallacy of composition: since a C in history is less than a B, the child is “failing” history and since the child is falling history, they are failing their parents standards. What is true for one part of the whole must be true for the whole. If one aspect of a person or product is bad, then the person or product as a whole is bad. This is an incorrect statement because most people or products exist in a gray area: they do some things well but not everything.

content.heading: Closed source and nuance
paragraphs: Going back to the hacker community, some hackers will dismiss Bambu Lab 3d printers as an option entirely simply because they run closed source software, disregarding the dozens of quality of life improvements over other brands. For most beginners and even engineers, those convenience features offer a better value proposition over other brands which is why people end up buying them, something the open-source-or-death proponents dismiss as being “fanboys”, with nearly the exact same situation playing out for Mac laptops and Unity Engine.

To put it bluntly, these people are wrong. Closed source software, or any single aspect of a product, while possibly being a deal breaker for some, shouldn’t define the entire product. I personally do not use Godot engine because I find Unity much easier to use, but when people inquire about what engine they should start with, it’s often my top recommendation because I can see that what’s best for me isn’t the same as what’s best for everyone. The day the hacker community is able to recognize this is the day they begin to be taken seriously by the rest of the world.

figures.text: Hackathons are definitely one of the most resourceful events you can run. For beginners, they provide a great opportunity to learn programming. They’re a great way for companies to connect with student developers and perform outreach while offering an educational event entirely free of charge. They can become game jams to cater toward artists and sound designers on top of programmers and the scale of the event can be as narrow as a single school to as wide as the entire globe.

But hackathons also come with their unique challenges. Being primarily funded by corporate sponsors demands outreach tables, keynotes, and sometimes custom tracks or prize categories, all of which make an event feel more corporate and less human. And satellite events, 200+ events that are run simultaneously across the globe, events that share the same website, branding, schedule, and theming, apart from location, how do you differentiate one from another?

Running events is all about resolving problems, and so the best event organizers aren’t the ones who can come up with good ideas, but the ones that can come up with quick fixes to onsite issues.I was the lead organizer for Campfire San Jose and today we’re diving into how event planners solved some of the most widespread, endemic problems plaguing hackathons around the globe.

figures.title: Participant satisfaction
figures.description: 20 points over global average

figures.title: Identifying the problem
figures.text: Before Campfire, there was Daydream, a very similar event structure but with different branding. I was involved in organizing Daydream, but Campfire was my first time leading an event team, and to be entirely honest, I didn’t know what the heck I was doing. My planning philosophy going into this was to solve problems, and one of the questions I asked when recruiting organizers was “what problem do you see at most hackathons and how can it be solved?” Of the responses that I read, one issue kept coming up: workshops.

Citing low attendance and participant interest, some organizers wanted to cut all workshops except for the ones absolutely required. Other organizers argued that hackathons were already not very beginner friendly and running more workshops was the way to go. It’s physically impossible to run both more and less workshops at the same time, but it does make you think, does the issue really lie in the number of workshops?
e

figures.title: Godot
items: Opens source beginner friendly game engine

figures.title: Polish
items: Final touches to make a game great

figures.title: Shipping
items: Exporting games to a playable state

figures.text: It’s impossible to get everything right when running an event, which is why it’s so important to make improvements between iterations. And it’s even more important to know what problems exist so you know what to improve on, and one of the easiest ways to do this is to ask the participants themselves.

figures.title: Approach 1: Listen to participant feedback
figures.text: Prizes for Daydream Global, the online global version of Daydream, as well as every official Hack Club hackathon at the time, were determined entirely by peer voting which while eliminating organizer and judge bias, introduced new problems of its own. Peer voting, at best, is a flawed project judging system for hackathons and oh I see you, before you start typing those hate comments, you need to understand there’s a fundamental difference between electoral democracy and hackathon peer voting.

In a democratic voting system, you have a large population of millions of people and a select few candidates to choose from. This makes it relatively easy to research all candidates and make an accurate choice based on your personal situation. I say relatively because that doesn’t always happen, as illustrated by the 2024 presidential election.

But hackathon peer voting is a different scenario entirely. Not only is the voter-candidate ratio blown up significantly, because the voters are also the candidates, they have a personal incentive to vote unfairly. Participants would “trade votes” with each other to increase their own chance of winning. Some would go as far to spam low reviews on every project in order to decrease others’ ratings relative to theirs. This obviously makes for a very unfair and biased judging system, so the events team made a change. .

In a democratic voting system, you have a large population of millions of people and a select few candidates to choose from. This makes it relatively easy to research all candidates and make an accurate choice based on your personal situation. I say relatively because that doesn’t always happen, as illustrated by the 2024 presidential election.

figures.title: Approach 2: Increase margin of error
figures.text: Delays are the final boss of events and hackathons are no exception. Wifi can break, food may not arrive on time, and project presenters can run into technical difficulties. When I see on the official schedule that a robotics competition will end at 5:30, I tell my parents to pick me up at 7, and they usually arrive just as the event ends. But the way events handle these delays can completely change the final outcome. At MakerHacks 1, the organizers significantly underestimated the time it takes to run robotics matches, and the event ended nearly an hour behind schedule. In a schedule with no flexibility, one event getting delayed leads to a cascading effect of every event afterward being delayed, worsening the problem until you end with a closing ceremony hours after the originally scheduled time.

While time can be regained by cutting other events, an ideal schedule includes built in delay buffers to reduce the effect any individual delay has on the event as a whole. For Campfire San Jose, both of our day-of schedules included an hour of delay buffer for the inevitable case that a mini-event runs behind schedule, and this proved extremely useful. On the first day, our primary schedule ended perfectly on time with everyone out of the building by 8. However, we did underestimate potential delays on the second day, when the actual voting and prize ceremony takes place. But while the final closing ceremony was a bit rushed, no events were cut due to delays and we exited the building on time both days. Simply by anticipating issues ahead of time and having a margin of error, we were able to reduce a potential hours-long delay down to just the final closing ceremony. And no, this isn’t an exaggeration. A campfire event in another location ended three hours behind schedule when their venue finally kicked them out.

figures.question: Do older posts need to be rewritten immediately?
figures.answer: No. Older section-based content is converted into text cards automatically, so existing posts keep working.

figures.question: Can one post mix multiple figure types in one article?
figures.answer: Yes. The showcase above combines prose, images, charts, stats, quotes, comparisons, embeds, a timeline, and Q and A in a single post.
